Judea was prosperous in the days of Hezekiah, but the kingdom of Christ is the main subject. Converting grace makes a soul that was once a wilderness rejoice with joy and singing, and blossom abundantly. The weak and faint-hearted are encouraged. This is the purpose of the gospel. Fear weakens; the more we strive against it, the stronger we become, both for doing and suffering; and he who says to us, Be strong, has provided help for us in One who is mighty. Assurance is given that Messiah will come to take vengeance on the powers of darkness, to comfort abundantly those who mourn in Zion; He will come and save. He will come again at the end of time to punish those who have troubled his people; and to give those who were troubled such rest as will be a complete reward for all their troubles.

When Christ comes to set up his kingdom in the world, then great wonders will be worked on people's souls. By the word and Spirit of Christ, spiritually blind people were enlightened; and those deaf to the calls of God were made to hear them readily. Those unable to do any good by Divine grace were made active therein. Those who did not know how to speak of God or to God had their lips opened to show forth his praise. When the Holy Spirit came upon the Gentiles that heard the word, then were the fountains of life opened. Most of the earth is still a desert; neither means of grace, spiritual worshippers, nor fruits of holiness are found in it. But the way of religion and godliness will be laid open. The way of holiness is the way of God’s commandment; it is the good old way. And the way to heaven is a plain way. Those knowing little and unlearned will be kept from missing the road. It will be a safe way; nothing can do them any real hurt. Christ, the way to God, will be clearly made known; and the way of a believer’s duty will be plainly marked out. Let us then go forward cheerfully, assured that the end of this way will be everlasting joy and rest for the soul. Those who by faith are made citizens of the gospel Zion rejoice in Christ Jesus; and their sorrows and sighs flee away by Divine consolations. Thus these prophecies conclude. Our joyful hopes and prospects of eternal life should overshadow all the sorrows and all the joys of this present time. But what is it to admire the excellence of God’s word unless we can claim its precious promises as our own? Do we love God, not only as our Creator, but because he gave his only Son to die for us? And are we walking in the ways of holiness? Let us try ourselves by such plain questions rather than spend time on things that may be curious and amusing, but are unprofitable.
